title: The Fury of Being Talked Over in Every Meeting
dek: *That hot flash of rage when someone cuts you off mid-sentence is not a character flaw. It is your organism defending the fundamental right to be heard, a biological protest against invisibility that carries information about power, respect, and the subtle violence of being repeatedly silenced in rooms where decisions get made.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Being talked over activates a specific nervous system cascade that your body reads as social erasure, not mere rudeness. The fury you feel is protective intelligence signaling that your voice and presence matter in the hierarchy of attention. Learning to work with this anger rather than suppress it strengthens both your boundaries and your capacity for clear communication.

You have been taught that anger is the problem. That if you could just stay calm, breathe deeply, and let it roll off your back, you would be the bigger person. But what if the real problem is not your fury at being interrupted for the third time in a single meeting, but a culture that has convinced you that rage at disrespect is less professional than the disrespect itself? When someone talks over you, your body registers a threat. Not the kind that requires you to run from a predator, but the subtler, more corrosive threat of social negation. The amygdala does not distinguish between a tiger in the grass and a colleague who has just steamrolled your half-finished sentence to claim your idea as his own. Both signal danger. Both demand a response. In our experience, the people who struggle most with meeting interruptions are the ones who have been socialized to believe their anger is inappropriate. Women, especially. People of color. Anyone who has learned that expressing displeasure might cost them safety, opportunity, or belonging. You have been handed a double bind: speak up and risk being labeled aggressive, or stay silent and disappear. ## The Physiology of Being Erased Your heart rate spikes. Heat floods your chest and neck. Your jaw tightens. You might notice your hands curling into fists under the table, or a sudden pressure behind your eyes. This is not drama. This is your autonomic nervous system responding to a violation of social contract. PubMed research on anger expression and cardiovascular load demonstrates that suppressing anger while experiencing provocation increases blood pressure and heart rate more than either expressing anger or experiencing no provocation at all. Your body pays a metabolic price for swallowing your rage. The interruption itself lasts only seconds, but the internal cascade can persist for twenty minutes or more. Cortisol levels rise. Your prefrontal cortex, the part of your brain responsible for nuanced thinking and strategic response, goes partially offline. You are now operating from a more primitive neural architecture, one designed for survival rather than workplace diplomacy. This is why you cannot always think of the perfect response in the moment. Your brain is not malfunctioning. It is doing exactly what it evolved to do when social standing is threatened: prioritize immediate defense over clever rhetoric. ## The Cumulative Weight of Small Erasures One interruption in one meeting might be forgiven as carelessness. But you are not dealing with one interruption. You are dealing with a pattern, a repetition that has trained your nervous system to expect violation. Over the years I've seen how chronic interruption creates a kind of hypervigilance, where you enter every meeting braced for the moment your voice will be taken from you. Beverly Engel, who has written extensively on the relationship between anger and boundary violations, notes that anger often arises not from a single incident but from accumulated experiences of being diminished. Each interruption adds to an invisible ledger. Your fury is not disproportionate. It is proportionate to the total weight of being talked over, not just today but across months or years. The body keeps score in ways your conscious mind might dismiss. You start to notice a tightness in your shoulders before meetings. You rehearse what you will say with an intensity that feels excessive, because some part of you knows that you will need to fight for the space to say it. You might find yourself speaking faster, rushing to get your point out before the interruption comes, which paradoxically makes interruption more likely. This is the trap. The anticipation of being silenced begins to shape your speech patterns, your posture, your presence. You are no longer just responding to interruptions. You are organizing your entire communication style around the expectation of them. ## What Your Anger Knows That You Do Not Anger is information. It tells you where your boundaries are and when they have been crossed. The fury you feel when talked over is not evidence of poor emotional regulation. It is evidence that you know, at a bone-deep level, that you deserve to be heard. Thomas Moore, in his work on the soul's relationship to darker emotions, suggests that anger often contains a sacred protest against conditions that violate human dignity. Your rage is not petty. It is responding to something real: the systematic devaluation of your contribution, your expertise, your right to occupy space in a conversation. When you dismiss your anger as overreaction, you dismiss the intelligence it carries. You might tell yourself it does not matter, that you are being too sensitive, that you should just let it go. But your body disagrees. Your body is saying: this matters. Your voice matters. The ideas you did not get to finish matter. Listening to this intelligence does not mean you must shout or storm out of meetings. It means you must stop pretending the anger is baseless. It means you must treat your fury as a messenger bringing urgent news about conditions that need to change. ## The Practice: Naming the Pattern in Real Time Here is something you can do the next time you are interrupted. It takes less than ten seconds and requires no preparation. Pause. Make eye contact with the person who talked over you. Say calmly: "I was not finished." Then continue exactly where you left off. You do not need to smile. You do not need to soften it with "sorry" or "just quickly." You do not need to explain why you deserve to complete your sentence. You simply name the fact that you were interrupted and reclaim the floor. This is not aggression. This is boundary maintenance. You are teaching the room that your speech has integrity, that it cannot be casually dismantled without consequence. Most people will immediately back down. Some will apologize. A few will bristle, which tells you something useful about their relationship to other people's boundaries. The first time you do this, your heart will pound. Your voice might shake. That is fine. You are rewiring years of conditioning that taught you to absorb interruption silently. Your nervous system needs evidence that speaking up will not end in catastrophe. Each time you name the pattern, you provide that evidence. ## When Anger Needs a Container Sometimes the fury is too large for a single calm statement. You leave the meeting shaking, replaying the interruption, imagining all the things you should have said. Your body is still flooded with activation that has nowhere to go. This is when you need physical discharge. Not immediately, not in the meeting itself, but soon. Go to the bathroom and splash cold water on your wrists and the back of your neck. If you can step outside, walk fast enough that your breath quickens. Clench and release your fists ten times. Make sound in your car on the drive home, even if it is just a long sustained growl. The anger needs to move through your body, not just be thought about. Rumination keeps the activation cycling without resolution. Physical action, even small action, signals to your nervous system that the threat response can complete. Some people find that [Herb Pharm Stress Manager (1 oz)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005JW1F1C?tag=spankyspinola-20) helps take the edge off the physiological intensity without numbing the emotional clarity. You are not trying to erase the anger. You are trying to metabolize it so it does not corrode you from the inside. For longer-term nervous system support, particularly if you are dealing with chronic meeting stress, [Banyan Botanicals Gotu Kola Powder (1 lb)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0058AADU4?tag=spankyspinola-20) has been used traditionally to support mental clarity and resilience under pressure. Think of these as tools in a larger practice of tending to your system, not quick fixes for justified rage. ## The Difference Between Assertion and Aggression You have been told that any expression of anger in professional settings is aggression. This is a lie designed to keep you quiet. Assertion is stating your needs, your boundaries, your reality without apology. Aggression is attempting to dominate or harm another person. When you say "I was not finished," you are asserting. When you raise your voice to the point of intimidation or use language designed to shame, you are moving toward aggression. Robert Augustus Masters, who has written extensively on the conscious expression of difficult emotions, distinguishes between anger that is in service of clarity and anger that is in service of revenge. The first kind seeks to change conditions. The second kind seeks to punish. You can feel the difference in your body. Assertion wants resolution. Aggression wants destruction. Most of us have been so starved of permission to assert that we confuse any firm boundary with violence. We have watched men express anger freely while women who do the same are called shrill or difficult. We have seen how tone policing is used as a weapon against people who dare to name mistreatment. The answer is not to modulate yourself into invisibility. The answer is to practice assertion until you can distinguish it from the aggression you fear you might become. This takes time. It takes making mistakes. It takes learning that you can be firm and direct without being cruel, that you can hold your ground without needing to demolish the other person. ## The Interruption Economy Meetings run on attention. Attention is finite. In any conversation, there is only so much space. When someone interrupts you, they are not just being rude. They are claiming a resource: the room's focus, the decision-making power that comes from being heard, the social capital of having contributed the idea that moves the project forward. This is why interruption is not evenly distributed. Research on meeting dynamics consistently shows that women are interrupted more than men, and that this gap widens in male-dominated fields. People with marginalized identities report being talked over at higher rates than their colleagues with more social power. Your anger recognizes what polite discourse tries to obscure: interruption is a power move. It is a way of establishing hierarchy. It communicates, without ever saying so explicitly, whose voice matters and whose can be overridden. When you understand this, you stop taking it personally in the way that implies you did something wrong. You start seeing it as a structural issue that requires structural response. This does not make the fury less intense. But it does make it more useful, because you can channel it toward changing patterns rather than just managing your own feelings in isolation. Across the articles we've published on this site, one theme emerges again and again: anger becomes destructive when it has no outlet, no pathway to change. Anger becomes generative when it is linked to action, even small action, that shifts the conditions that produced it. ## Practical Responses That Preserve Your Energy You cannot call out every interruption. Some battles are not worth the metabolic cost. You need a graduated response system, a way to decide which interruptions to address directly and which to note and move past. Minor interruptions from someone who generally respects your contributions might warrant a simple "Let me finish this thought" followed by immediate continuation. Chronic interruptions from the same person require a different approach: a private conversation outside the meeting where you name the pattern and ask for change. If you are being systematically talked over in meetings and the culture does not shift when you assert yourself, you are dealing with a bigger problem than interruption. You are dealing with an environment that does not value your contribution. At that point, the question is not how to manage your anger better. The question is whether this environment deserves your continued presence. Sometimes the most powerful thing you can do with your fury is let it inform a decision to leave. Not in a blaze of dramatic exit, but in a clear-eyed recognition that you are worth more than an endless fight for basic respect. Your anger is telling you something about fit, about where your gifts can actually land, about which rooms are built to contain you and which are designed to keep you small. For those dealing with the physical toll of ongoing workplace stress, [Banyan Botanicals Triphala Guggulu](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000Q48DGS?tag=spankyspinola-20) is sometimes used to support metabolic balance and healthy stress response. But no supplement can substitute for changing the conditions that are grinding you down. ## Building Alliances in the Interruption Wars You do not have to fight this alone. In any meeting, there are likely others who are also being talked over, whose contributions are also being minimized. There is power in witnessing for each other. When you see someone else interrupted, you can say: "Hold on, I want to hear the rest of what Sarah was saying." When someone tries to claim your idea as their own, an ally can say: "That is what Jordan proposed ten minutes ago. Let's give them credit." This kind of mutual support changes meeting culture faster than individual assertion alone. Building these alliances requires trust. You need to have conversations outside the meeting about what you are each experiencing, about patterns you have noticed, about how you want to support each other. This is not complaining. This is organizing. You might also find useful frameworks and reflection tools in [the assessments hub](/assessments), where you can get clearer on your own anger patterns and communication style. Sometimes what feels like pure fury in the moment becomes more workable when you understand the larger emotional architecture you are operating within. The [fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) offers additional practices for working with intense activation, especially when you need to move through anger quickly in order to function. These are not about suppression. They are about having options, about being able to choose your response rather than being hijacked by the intensity of the moment. ## When the Anger Reveals Something Deeper Sometimes the disproportionate fury you feel at being interrupted is not just about the meeting. It is about every time you were told to be quiet as a child. Every time your expertise was questioned in ways your male colleague's never was. Every time you made yourself small to avoid conflict. The interruption in the meeting becomes a portal into older wounds, unresolved experiences of being silenced. This does not mean your current anger is invalid. It means it is carrying more than just the present moment. It is carrying history. When you notice this layering, you need both immediate response to the present situation and longer-term work with the older material. The immediate response looks like the practices already described: naming the interruption, asserting your boundary, completing your thought. The longer-term work might involve therapy, somatic practice, or the kind of deep self-inquiry that [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) explores in its focus on emotional archaeology and personal mythology. You cannot heal all of your history in the middle of a budget meeting. But you can begin to distinguish between the anger that belongs to this moment and the anger that belongs to a lifetime of similar moments. That distinction gives you more freedom in how you respond. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### What if I interrupt people too and my anger feels hypocritical? Most people interrupt sometimes. The question is whether you do it systematically to the same people, whether you notice when you do it, and whether you are willing to be called on it. Hypocrisy would be interrupting others while raging at being interrupted yourself without any self-awareness. Complexity is recognizing that you are working on this pattern in yourself while also refusing to absorb chronic interruption from others. You can hold both. Your anger at being talked over can coexist with your commitment to interrupt less. They are not in conflict. ### How do I handle being told I am too sensitive about interruptions? When someone tells you that you are too sensitive, they are attempting to make your boundary the problem instead of their behavior. The appropriate response is not to argue about whether you are sensitive. It is to restate the boundary: "Whether or not I am sensitive is not the issue. The issue is that I was not finished speaking." You do not need their agreement that your feelings are valid in order to assert that you deserve to complete your sentences. Sensitivity is not weakness. It is accurate perception of what is happening. ### What if the person who keeps interrupting me is my boss? This is harder because the power differential is real. You cannot address it the same way you would with a peer. Options include: speaking to your boss privately about the pattern, documenting instances for potential HR involvement if it is part of a larger hostile environment, building alliances with colleagues who can help strengthen your contributions, and frankly assessing whether this job is sustainable if the pattern does not change. Sometimes the answer is that your boss is not going to change and you need to decide if you can live with that or if you need to find an environment where your voice is valued. Your anger is giving you information about that decision. ## The Long View Anger about being interrupted is not a problem to solve. It is a relationship to tend. Some days you will handle it with grace. Some days you will leave the meeting shaking with rage you could not express. Some days you will speak up and it will change nothing. Some days you will speak up and watch the whole room shift. What matters is that you stop treating your fury as evidence of deficiency. What matters is that you learn to hear what it is trying to tell you about respect, about value, about the conditions under which you can do your best work. What matters is that you refuse the story that says your anger is the problem. There are more resources waiting for you. [More articles in this journal](/articles) explore different facets of working with difficult emotions in professional and personal contexts. You do not have to figure this out alone. You do not have to be perfect at it. You just have to keep insisting that your voice matters, that your contributions have weight, that the space you take up in a conversation is not something you need to apologize for. Your anger already knows this. Now you get to practice living like it is true. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on the fury of being talked over in every meeting, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
